evidence_quotes.quote: Games aren't things you own anymore, but are still sold to you as if they were.
These games that are download only now, force the user to agree to service agreements which are inherently ANTI CONSUMER.
they use dark patterns to force you to agree with their terms of service.
the worst offense is where these companies say that you only own a license to play, and don't own the game and therefore are allowed to do these things

paragraphs.text: Dear Sir/Madam/x, I would like the Digital Fairness Act to include the points of the "Stop Killing Games initiative". See: https://stopkillinggames.wiki.gg/ My personal motivation is as followed: I am a life long gamer. I and many other gamers have paid thousands of euros in building my game library. In the old days before digital licenses were the norm, games were playable from the disc it came on. As games have grown in size and complexity, the format dictates games can be updated by the developer/publisher. The danger in this shift in how games are "sold", is in the anti consumer practises that follow. Games aren't things you own anymore, but are still sold to you as if they were. Games are digital products that can almost always ONLY be downloaded at a digital store. Steam, the Playstation store or the Microsoft store are a few examples. These games that are download only now, force the user to agree to service agreements which are inherently ANTI CONSUMER. Games are no longer a physical thing you own, now they are licenses that grant you the right to play, untill the publisher/developer decides you don't. THIS IS ANTI CONSUMER AND WRONG. If I buy a movie on a blu ray disc, the license agreement is that I can only use it for personal usage, but even if the company that produces the blurays, the production company goes bankrupt, or the publisher or distributor stops existing, I can still play the movie. Nowadays with digital games, the format dictates that games can be killed after something happens to the companies responsible for developing and distributing them or if they simply choose to against your will, because they use dark patterns to force you to agree with their terms of service. All new games sold in the EU should have an "end of life plan" where if a developer or publisher of that material wants to stop supporting certain functions of the game, due to it being old and no longer viable to support, the game should be by EU law forced to be left in a playable state. If a game has a multiplayer mode, which runs through the internet and the publisher's servers, they should for instance allow the community to make their own servers. I do not want developers/publishers to go bankrupt because they have to keep servers alive forever, but there needs to be a plan where alternatives are thought of during the development of the game, so the game remains playable, long after the game is stopped being supported. For single player games and game modes however, these companies have also used the same dark patterns to make you agree to their terms of service, where games that are only played locally on the users pc, Sony Playstation (3, 4, 5, 5 pro ~), Microsoft XBOX (one, one s, one x, series s, series x ~), Nintendo Switch (1 and 2) as an example, which the publisher/developer can also make unplayable WHENEVER they want. The worst offense is where these companies say that you only own a license to play, and don't own the game and therefore are allowed to do these things, which I personally hate and find anti consumer. When you look on their stores (Sony Playstation Store, Microsoft Store, Steam, EA store etc, they use the term "buy" but in reality, this is false advertising, because you effectively rent it, EVEN IF YOU "BUY" a physical copy in a store. They use buy, but you just rent the game untill they (developer/publisher etc) decide they don't want to support it because this is unregulated and like the initiative of "stop killing games" I want to be able to play the games after the developer or publisher etc. doesn't want to support it.Publishers destroy video games they have sold to customers. An increasing number of video games are sold effectively as goods - with no stated expiration date - but designed to be completely unplayable as soon as support from the publisher ends. This practice is a form of planned obsolescence and is not only detrimental to customers, but makes preservation effectively impossible.
